“Big Jason” Henderson Crib notes
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 1 of 5 --

“Big Jason” Henderson Crib Notes
www.halbertology.com 	2
“Big Jason” Henderson – Volatile Email Marketing
What’s the best email service provider at the moment? Big Jason has been using Active
Campaign (www.activecampaignrocks.com) and has found it affective.
Will email marketing survive the social media onslaught? Big Jason thinks they’re totally
different animals, that they’re both great avenues for marketing in different ways, and it’s good
to have different channels open at once. He does a lot of tracking on social media, and a lot of
testing with email marketing.
How do smart phones change the way you email market? Design something simple yet
responsive that will resize on mobile or desktop. Make sure that you can read it on mobile, but
don’t necessarily cater to that too much.
Big Jason’s favorite font at the moment: Georgia, 16-18pt. zerb.com/blog gives some good tips
on formatting.
A lot of this is about the decision to read. Avoid eye fatigue by not making your column of text
too narrow or too wide (600-620 pixels across should be the max, make sure images don’t take
up more than 100% of the length of the screen even if it’s on mobile). Make an alt link to entice
people into loading your images.
What’s the latest on spam rules? Generally the same. It’s bad to misspell words, it just
makes you look stupid. Your reputation is more important than the content, response is what
matters.
Tips on engagement: Getting your subscribers to reply to emails is important, it shows the
bigger email providers that you’re having an actual conversation and it builds a rapport with
your list. Add in a photo of yourself occasionally, make it more personal. Social media is a great
way to engage through contests, comments, etc.
It’s better to be in the spam folder of a primary email address than the primary folder of a spam
email – especially since the primary email is the one that will be on mobile.The mindset you
should have is this: how do I get more qualified people to open and click through? You have to
imagine what might be stopping people from reading their email. Real Curiosity + Relevant
Benefit = Opens.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 2 of 5 --

“Big Jason” Henderson Crib Notes
www.halbertology.com 	3
Personalization works only in some situations, a lot of the time it’s to gain attention. You could
use their first name, but also the city they’re from, etc.Email can be some of your most personal
form of contact with your clients after they buy.
Big Jason is tracking: Unique opens, unique click-throughs, revenue per email, revenue per
click. Heat maps in emails can give some valuable information.
An open loop is like a cliffhanger in a TV show, teasing the reader to tune in next time. Don’t
use an open loop after you’ve made a call-to-action – because they’ll delay the action.
Welcome emails: share your personality. Encourage them to add you to their “whitelist” (the
emails that get in to their primary inbox). Replies are the ultimate way to do this – as simple as
“got it” or asking you their biggest question. Big Jason wrote one email from the perspective of
his rabbit – that kind of quirkiness can really add personality.
Timing your emails (when to send them): track and test when the best time is for your
list. 11pm works well, early evening, 4am-7am… depends on the people on your list.
Timing for auto-responders: how often you mail them should be dependent on how often
you want to hear from them. But make sure that they don’t forget who you are! The frequency
isn’t always there, most marketers don’t email enough. If there’s a good sales letter, there’s
plenty of fodder. He tends to use one big-picture bullet point per newsletter.
Big Jason uses very few formulas. Themes that open loops may work (especially for pre-launch
and engagement), but it depends on why the person initially signed up for your list.
Gifs in an email can be a great way to grab attention, adding motion to your emails. Can be just
a series of photos or a more fluid thing, like a video without sound.
What’s the ideal subject line length? Point-first or point-last, not point-middle where it gets
lost, but he doesn’t have a hard and fast rule for the length.
Binging on shows helps inspire him and give him ideas for emails!
Reactivation campaigns are great for getting dead parts of your list engaged. It can be ballsy,
but can definitely work in some cases. Use high-quality content to lure people in to buy.
Advice for newbies: Start a weekly show on Google Hangouts (already recorded), use Q&A
as fodder (can be as short as 15 minutes).
Contact: www.emailresponsewarrior.com
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 3 of 5 --

“Big Jason” Henderson Crib Notes

any communication from or between us, are for general information and educational purposes only. Certain
speakers and persons due to educate may or may not in some instances be able to do so in part or at all. You
acknowledge this and agree that no claim at all may be made against any such person or ourselves or our
partners affiliates agents and employees or any person in this regard.

accuracy, completeness or suitability of the information and materials found or offered on our websites
and/or materials and/or service for any particular purpose. You acknowledge that such information and
materials may contain errors or inaccuracies and we expressly exclude liability for any such inaccuracies or
errors to the fullest extent permitted by law.
We have made every effort to accurately represent this product and/or service offered and its potential.
Despite this there is no guarantee that you will earn any money using the techniques and ideas in these
materials or as a result of the services. Examples in materials are not to be interpreted as a promise or
guarantee of earnings. Earning potential is entirely dependent on the person using our product, ideas,
techniques and services. We do not purport this as a get rich quick scheme – the techniques may be proven
however their capacity to create wealth is based purely on the commitment, determination and willingness of
the individual(s) concerned.
Materials in our product and/or our website and/or services may contain information that includes, or is
based upon, forward-looking statements within the meaning of the securities litigation reform act of 1995.
Forward-looking statements give our expectations or forecasts of future events and are intended to express
our opinion of earnings potential. You can identify these statements by the fact that they do not relate strictly
to historical or current facts. Words such as “anticipate,” “project,” “estimate,” “expect,” “plan,” “intend,”
“believe,” and other words and terms of similar meaning in connection with a description of potential
earnings or financial performance might be used.
There are many factors that will be important in determining your actual results and no guarantees are made
that you will achieve results similar to ours or anybody else’s. In fact no guarantees are made that you will
achieve any results from our ideas and techniques in our material. All and earnings or income examples
provided are only estimates off what we think you could earn. At no time is there any assurance that you will
in fact also do so by replying on these figures.. We do not purport that any prior success or past results as to
income earnings are indicative of your future success or results.
Internet businesses and marketing and earnings derived there from may have unknown risks attached and
are not suitable for everyone and there is risk that one could experience significant losses or make no money
at all.
Business carries a high level of risk, and may not be suitable for all investors. Before deciding to set out on
any business venture, you should carefully consider your investment objectives, level of experience, and risk
appetite. The possibility exists that you could sustain a loss of some or all of your initial investment and
therefore, you should not invest money that you cannot afford to lose. You should be aware of all the risks
associated with a business, and seek advice if you have any doubts from an independent financial advisor
and/or qualified professionals such as lawyer or accountant before acting on any of this information.

decisions or any reliance made based on them. We are not to be held liable for the successor failure of your

We would never mean to say or pretend that any person will or is likely to achieve profits or loses similar to
those discussed on this website. Each person may have different results. In fact, it’s pretty certain they and
you will not have similar results and there are frequently sharp differences between hypothetical
performance results and actual results subsequently achieved by any particular business. So the saying "Past
performance is not indicative of future results" really holds true here.
One of the limitations of hypothetical performance results is that they are generally prepared with the benefit
of hindsight. Also, hypothetical trading does not involve financial risk and that’s why no hypothetical profit
record can completely account for the impact of financial risk in business.
There are a lot of other factors related to the market in general or to the implementation of any specific
business objective which cannot be fully accounted for in the preparation of hypothetical performance results
and all of which can adversely affect results.
Although we make every attempt to assure accuracy, we do not give any express or implied warranty as to its
accuracy. What we are saying here is that we do not accept any liability for any error or omission. You'll see
we have examples posted now and again. These are for illustrative purposes only so please do not be
construe them as investment advice or strategy.
